text: This is a video of laparoscopic rectopexy for rectal prolapse. This was a sixty three years old female who had undergone vaginal esrectomy one and a half years ago and she presented with rectal prolapse since eight months. On barian enema there was no sigmoid redundancy and colonoscopy was normal. So we planned a laparoscopic rectopexy for her.
The surgeon releases adhesions on the anterior aspect and lateral pelvic wall using scissors and monopolar current. Adhesions between the omentum and vaginal wall are also being carefully dissected.

The surgeon releases adhesions on the lateral pelvic wall and applies traction to the rectum towards the left side. Dissection is performed on both the posterior and anterior aspects of the rectum, opening the plane between the vaginal vault and the rectum. Fat is identified as belonging to the rectum, and the anterior plane is circumferentially dissected and joined with the posteriorly dissected plane, visibly separating the vaginal vault from the rectum.
The surgeon applies a pulsing technique to achieve approximately four to five centimeters of anterior mobility, allowing the rectum to be pulled upwards more efficiently.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, identifying the loose areolar tissue as the correct plane. Adequate traction is applied to the rectum to aid in plane identification. The hook and monopolar current are used to open up tissue planes, demonstrating their effectiveness over harmonic devices in this context.

The surgeon marks the surgical site to identify the correct dissection plane, entering the loose areolar tissue. Dissection proceeds from right to left, top to bottom, with careful preservation of the left ureter.

The surgeon continues dissection on the posterior aspect, developing the plane on the left side. Once adequate dissection is achieved posteriorly, the planes on the left side, posterior aspect, and anteriorly are joined together.

The surgeon joins the anterior and posterior planes, opening up the pre-sacral space. A polycrippling mesh, approximately ten centimeters in size, is positioned and fixed to the sacral promontory using absorbable tacks.
The surgeon closes the plane on the left aspect and proceeds to close the right side peritoneal port. The mesh is then fully retroperitonealized.
The surgeon uses non-absorbable sutures to secure the mesh, which is now completely retroperitonealized, aiding in pulling up the rectum.
Thanks for watching this video friends.
